title: Steady çay for a tense week
overview: A week of hard headlines — but for residents the practical picture is steadier: the lira is drifting rather than diving, Thursday's central-bank decision is expected to hold, and the rules that govern daily life are shifting slowly, not suddenly.

sections.heading: The big picture
sections.body: The US–Iran ceasefire has broken down and strikes have resumed across the region. Shipping continues through the Strait of Hormuz, but tension is high — if you're flying east or south, check your airline's alerts, as occasional reroutes are likely.

sections.heading: Türkiye
sections.body: An intense political season: Özgür Özel, removed as CHP leader by court order in May, told parliament he will found a new party. Expect large announced rallies in big cities — easy to plan around, worth planning around.

sections.heading: Money
sections.body: The lira sits near ₺47.2 to the dollar and above ₺53.5 to the euro. June inflation eased to 32.1%, the lowest since March, and the central bank is widely expected to hold its 37% rate on Thursday. Foreign-currency earners: an ordinary month. Lira earners: keep an eye on rent-renewal caps.

sections.heading: Migration & residency
sections.body: Roughly 700,000 Syrians have returned home since December 2024; about 2.2 million remain, and UNHCR expects up to a million returns this year. Housekeeping for everyone else: the residence-permit card fee rose to ₺964 in April, and financial thresholds now track 1.5× the net minimum wage.

sections.heading: Safety
sections.body: A magnitude-5.0 quake near Malatya on 18 July caused no reported major damage. Türkiye stays at U.S. advisory Level 2 overall, with border provinces in the southeast at Level 4. A good week to check the water and batteries in your quake kit.

closing: Çay's ready — read well, and check on a neighbour.
